children.value: Having a strict lifestyle, such as focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities, may improve students’ academic performance.
Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

children.value: Figure 1. Average Study Time by Grade Group Across Four Datasets

Study time was chosen as the first indicator of strict lifestyle because it shows the students’ investment and prioritization of learning. Across four datasets, there is a consistent pattern: students in high grade groups spend more time studying, while low grade groups spend the least. However, the relationship is not similar across subjects. In the dataset 3, studying Mathematics report slightly more study time than those studying Portuguese. This is due to different learning demands of two subjects, as mathematics often requires more structured problem-solving tactics, while language relies heavily on continuous exposure, self-practicing and application in reality (

).
Although higher study time is correlated with higher academic performance, study time alone does not determine academic success. It can still depend on learning strategies, quality, attention, and motivation of students (
Students with fewer absences tend to achieve higher academic performance.
Figure 2. Average Absences by Grade Group in Mathematics and Portuguese

Figure 2 shows a clear relationship between study discipline and academic performance when measuring how many days students’ absences in the class. In both Mathematics and Portuguese, students in high grade group show fewest average absences, while medium and low grade groups are absent more frequently.

investigate that absenteeism can lead to lower grades, as missing classes reduces learning opportunities and classroom engagement. Moreover, mathematics students report more absences than Portuguese students across all grade groups, indicating slight differences in academic discipline between two subjects.
Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes
Figure 3. Average Final Grade by Study Time and Paid Extra Classes

Figure 3 indicates that the relationship between extra classes and study time, and average of final grade differs between mathematics and Portuguese. For Mathematics, students who did not attend extra classes experienced a fluctuating pattern, whereas those attending extra classes showed a stable increase in study time with increased average grades. This is because extra classes or study time may not fully explain mathematical grades; their performance may also depend on math-solving tactics, learning strategies, and conceptual understanding (

). For Portuguese, students studying more without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them, while the latter showed a fluctuating pattern. This difference lies in the subject characteristics: mathematics requires teacher’s problem-solving methods and guidance, as well as concentration and comprehension from students (
), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice in reality will be more effective in improving students’ language skills (
Despite possibly affecting mental wellbeing, strictly follow intense study schedule brings fruitful result
Figure 4 Grade Distribution by Stress Level

Overall, data shows that high stress correlates with high grades. Since strict, intensive study schedules are a very plausible reason students report high stress (rather than e.g. financial stress or personal issues, given this is a student lifestyle dataset), this chart visually supports the idea that strict lifestyles pay off academically. This phenomenon can be explained by the concept of eustress, which is a type of positive stress that result in positive outcome, which stems from the Yerkes-Dodson Law stating that ‘‘increasing stress is beneficial to performance until some optimum level is reached…’’ (

) However, it is worth noting that grades spread quite widely in the high stress group, which is shown more clearly in the with-datapoints version, thus the pattern may not apply universally every time.
